front: Painting: a headless female statue in a surgical glove gestures toward a dark grey boiler-shaped mechanical figure
Painting: the central figure was inspired by a photo of a Sudanese corn bin; title comes from a German nursery rhyme
Painting: a red, blue, and orange dragon celebrates; Ernst called it 'a clumsy oaf which destroys everything'; inspired by Franco's victory
Artist: collage novels cut from Victorian pulp fiction; A Week of Kindness split into seven sections using Easter Island moai and lion heads
work: Collage Novels (A Week of Kindness / Une Semaine de Bonté, La Femme 100 Têtes)

front: Artist: La Femme 100 Têtes title is a double entendre for 'headless' or 'hundred-headed'; introduced alter ego Loplop
work: Collage Novels (A Week of Kindness / Une Semaine de Bonté, La Femme 100 Têtes)

front: Artist: invented frottage (rubbing pencil over rough surfaces) and grattage (scraping paint off canvas)
Artist: alter ego Loplop, 'Superior of the Birds'; possibly originated from his pet parrot dying the same day his sister was born
Artist: escaped Nazi Germany with the help of Peggy Guggenheim, who became his third wife
